scenario_results.reason: The lane produced a post-specific handoff package with picture-lock, turnover, version, and owner continuity rather than generic project notes.
Missing picture-lock and turnover prerequisites were treated as blockers rather than a routable handoff.
Duplicated ownership across mastering, localization, and delivery was surfaced explicitly and not collapsed into an assumed next owner.
Conflicting CPL, track-file, and delivery-manifest records were preserved as a versioning blocker and routed to the correct operational owners.
Creative and mastering approval authority stayed outside the lane, with boundary-safe rerouting instead of over-claiming signoff.
Caption-quality failures were handled as accessibility and acceptance blockers requiring caption-owner action.
The lane refused to absorb export, subtitle repair, upload, and resend execution work.
Conflicting schedules and stale ownership were kept as a routing problem instead of synthesized into a false schedule truth.
Missing destination and systems-of-record facts triggered low-confidence escalation rather than speculative routing.
The lane escalated the conflict between public baseline and destination-specific delivery rules instead of pretending the public baseline alone was sufficient.
Tenant-specific system adaptation preserved post-specific turnover, accessibility, and delivery evidence rather than erasing the public baseline.
Closure was only confirmed after destination-owner proof, blocker resolution, and accepted artifact completeness were explicit.

notes: All 12 scenarios passed against the standard 0.92 threshold while keeping the lane specific to post-production turnovers, versioning, accessibility artifacts, QC, and delivery routing.
Functionality mapping and scenario coverage include all shared abilities, all coordination-handoffs-and-routing abilities, and the required cross-industry ability.
